content: Backpropagation computes gradients via the chain rule: dL/dw_i = dL/da_n * da_n/dz_n * dz_n/da_{n-1} * ... * da_i/dz_i * dz_i/dw_i, where a is activation, z is pre-activation (linear), and L is loss. For a network with n layers, this chains n multiplications. Each layer's gradient is computed in reverse order (output to input) and cached via a computational graph, making gradient computation O(n) rather than O(n^2).

Vanishing gradients occur when sigmoid/tanh activation derivatives (max 0.25 for sigmoid, 1.0 for tanh) are multiplied across many layers, causing gradients to shrink exponentially toward zero. A 10-layer network with sigmoid has gradients ~0.25^10 = 9.5e-7 at the first layer. ReLU (max(0,x)) has gradient 1 for positive inputs, solving vanishing gradients but introducing dying ReLU (neurons stuck at 0). Leaky ReLU uses a small slope (0.01x) for negative inputs.

Exploding gradients cause weights to diverge to infinity during training, manifesting as NaN losses. Gradient clipping caps the gradient norm: if ||g|| > threshold, g = g * threshold / ||g||. Typical threshold: 1.0-5.0. PyTorch: torch.nn.utils.clip_grad_norm_(model.parameters(), max_norm=1.0). LSTM/GRU architectures inherently mitigate exploding gradients through their gating mechanisms, which bound the gradient flow.

Batch normalization normalizes each mini-batch to zero mean and unit variance: x_hat = (x - mu_B) / sqrt(sigma_B^2 + epsilon), then scales and shifts: y = gamma * x_hat + beta, where gamma and beta are learnable parameters. During training, mu_B and sigma_B are per-batch; during inference, running averages are used. BN enables higher learning rates (3-10x), reduces sensitivity to initialization, and acts as a regularizer.

Layer normalization normalizes across features (not batch): mu and sigma computed per sample across all features. Unlike batch norm, it works with batch_size=1 and varying sequence lengths, making it standard in transformers. RMSNorm (used in LLaMA) simplifies further: x_hat = x / RMS(x) * gamma, where RMS(x) = sqrt(mean(x^2) + epsilon), removing the mean subtraction step for ~10% faster computation with equivalent performance.

Dropout randomly zeroes neuron outputs with probability p during training: mask ~ Bernoulli(1-p), output = mask * activation / (1-p). The 1/(1-p) scaling (inverted dropout) ensures expected activation magnitude is preserved. Standard p=0.5 for hidden layers, p=0.2 for input layers. At inference, all neurons are active (no dropout). Dropout approximates training an ensemble of 2^n sub-networks where n is the number of dropout-eligible neurons.

Learning rate schedules control lr over training epochs. Step decay: lr *= 0.1 every 30 epochs. Cosine annealing: lr_t = lr_min + 0.5*(lr_max - lr_min)*(1 + cos(pi*t/T)). Warmup: linearly increase lr from 0 to target over first 5-10% of steps, then decay. OneCycleLR: warmup → high lr → cosine decay, often achieving best results in fewer epochs. ReduceLROnPlateau: reduce lr by factor (0.1-0.5) when validation loss stalls for patience epochs.

Adam optimizer maintains per-parameter first moment (mean) m_t = beta1*m_{t-1} + (1-beta1)*g_t and second moment (variance) v_t = beta2*v_{t-1} + (1-beta2)*g_t^2. Bias-corrected: m_hat = m_t/(1-beta1^t), v_hat = v_t/(1-beta2^t). Update: theta -= lr * m_hat / (sqrt(v_hat) + epsilon). Defaults: beta1=0.9, beta2=0.999, epsilon=1e-8. AdamW decouples weight decay from gradient update, fixing L2 regularization behavior.

Xavier/Glorot initialization sets weights from N(0, 2/(fan_in + fan_out)) or Uniform(-sqrt(6/(fan_in+fan_out)), sqrt(6/(fan_in+fan_out))). This preserves variance across layers for tanh/sigmoid activations. He/Kaiming initialization uses N(0, 2/fan_in) for ReLU, accounting for the fact that ReLU zeroes negative half of the distribution. Wrong initialization causes training to diverge or converge extremely slowly.

ResNet skip connections add the input directly to the output of a residual block: y = F(x) + x, where F is typically 2-3 conv layers. This enables training 100+ layer networks by allowing gradients to flow through the identity shortcut, bypassing problematic layers. The key insight: learning residual F(x) = H(x) - x is easier than learning H(x) directly, because when the optimal mapping is near-identity, F(x) approaches zero.

Vision Transformers (ViT) split images into 16x16 patches, project each to an embedding vector, add positional embeddings, and process through standard transformer encoder layers. A 224x224 image becomes 196 patch tokens (14x14 grid). ViT requires large-scale pretraining (ImageNet-21k or JFT-300M) to outperform CNNs; with limited data, CNNs' inductive biases (locality, translation equivariance) give them an advantage.

LSTM gates control information flow: forget gate f_t = sigmoid(W_f*[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_f) decides what to erase from cell state; input gate i_t = sigmoid(W_i*[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_i) decides what new information to store; candidate c_tilde = tanh(W_c*[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_c) proposes new cell values; cell update c_t = f_t*c_{t-1} + i_t*c_tilde; output gate o_t = sigmoid(W_o*[h_{t-1},x_t]+b_o); hidden state h_t = o_t*tanh(c_t). The additive cell update avoids vanishing gradients over long sequences.

GRU (Gated Recurrent Unit) simplifies LSTM with 2 gates instead of 3: reset gate r_t = sigmoid(W_r*[h_{t-1},x_t]) controls how much past to forget; update gate z_t = sigmoid(W_z*[h_{t-1},x_t]) blends old and new state; candidate h_tilde = tanh(W*[r_t*h_{t-1},x_t]); output h_t = (1-z_t)*h_{t-1} + z_t*h_tilde. GRU matches LSTM performance on most tasks with ~33% fewer parameters and faster training.

Transfer learning typically follows: (1) take a model pretrained on large dataset (ImageNet, WebText), (2) freeze all layers except the final classification head, (3) train the head on your data for 5-10 epochs, (4) optionally unfreeze top N layers and fine-tune with 10-100x lower learning rate. Discriminative learning rates (lower lr for earlier layers, higher for later layers) improve fine-tuning. Feature extraction (frozen backbone) works with <1000 samples; full fine-tuning benefits from 5000+.

Data augmentation for images: random horizontal flip, rotation (+-15 degrees), color jitter (brightness/contrast/saturation +-20%), random crop and resize, Cutout (random square mask), Mixup (blend two images: x = lambda*x1 + (1-lambda)*x2, same for labels), CutMix (replace random patch with another image's patch). For text: back-translation, synonym replacement, random insertion/deletion, and paraphrase generation. Augmentation can be equivalent to 2-5x more training data.

Focal loss addresses class imbalance in object detection: FL(p_t) = -alpha_t * (1-p_t)^gamma * log(p_t). The (1-p_t)^gamma factor down-weights easy examples (high p_t) and focuses on hard examples (low p_t). With gamma=2, a well-classified example with p_t=0.9 has 100x lower loss than a misclassified example with p_t=0.1. Standard cross-entropy treats all examples equally, causing easy negatives to dominate the gradient in imbalanced datasets.

Contrastive learning (SimCLR, MoCo) learns representations by pulling augmented views of the same image together and pushing different images apart in embedding space. The InfoNCE loss: L = -log(exp(sim(z_i,z_j)/tau) / sum_k(exp(sim(z_i,z_k)/tau))) where tau is temperature (typically 0.07-0.5). Larger batch sizes provide more negatives and improve performance. Self-supervised contrastive pretraining matches or exceeds supervised pretraining when fine-tuned.

Knowledge distillation trains a smaller student model to mimic a larger teacher model's soft predictions. The distillation loss: L = alpha*T^2*KL(softmax(z_s/T), softmax(z_t/T)) + (1-alpha)*CE(y, softmax(z_s)), where T is temperature (2-20, higher = softer), alpha balances distillation and hard-label loss. Soft targets carry more information than hard labels because they encode inter-class similarities. Student can achieve 95%+ of teacher performance at 1/10th the size.

Gradient accumulation simulates larger batch sizes on limited GPU memory: for i, batch in enumerate(dataloader): loss = model(batch) / accumulation_steps; loss.backward(); if (i+1) % accumulation_steps == 0: optimizer.step(); optimizer.zero_grad(). With accumulation_steps=4 and batch_size=8, effective batch size is 32. Important: batch normalization statistics are still computed per micro-batch, which can degrade performance. Use gradient accumulation with layer norm or group norm instead.

Mixed precision training uses float16 for forward/backward passes and float32 for weight updates and loss scaling. PyTorch AMP: with torch.cuda.amp.autocast(): output = model(input); loss = criterion(output); scaler.scale(loss).backward(); scaler.step(optimizer). Loss scaling prevents float16 underflow of small gradients by multiplying loss by a large factor (initially 2^16) and dividing gradients back. Provides ~2x speedup and ~50% memory reduction on modern GPUs.

Attention mechanisms in CNNs (SE-Net, CBAM) learn to reweight feature channels. Squeeze-and-Excitation: globally average pool each channel to a scalar, pass through FC→ReLU→FC→Sigmoid to get channel weights, multiply original features by these weights. This adds <1% parameters but improves accuracy by 0.5-1% on ImageNet. CBAM adds spatial attention (where to focus) on top of channel attention (what to focus on).

Depthwise separable convolutions (MobileNet) factorize standard convolution into depthwise (one filter per channel) and pointwise (1x1 conv across channels). A standard 3x3 conv on 64→128 channels costs 3*3*64*128 = 73,728 multiplications per position. Depthwise separable: 3*3*64 + 64*128 = 8,768 multiplications, an 8.4x reduction. This enables efficient models running at 30+ FPS on mobile devices with <5M parameters.

Label smoothing replaces hard one-hot targets with softened targets: y_smooth = (1-epsilon)*y_hard + epsilon/K, where K is the number of classes and epsilon is typically 0.1. For a 10-class problem with epsilon=0.1, the target becomes 0.91 for correct class and 0.01 for each wrong class. This prevents the model from becoming overconfident, improving calibration and generalization by 0.2-0.5% on classification benchmarks.

Early stopping monitors validation loss and halts training when it stops improving for 'patience' epochs (typically 5-20). Restore weights from the best epoch. Combined with learning rate reduction on plateau: reduce lr by 0.5 when val_loss stalls for 5 epochs, stop training when val_loss stalls for 15 epochs even after lr reductions. This prevents overfitting without requiring precise epoch count selection.

Diffusion models generate data by learning to reverse a gradual noising process. Forward process adds Gaussian noise in T steps: x_t = sqrt(alpha_bar_t)*x_0 + sqrt(1-alpha_bar_t)*epsilon. The model learns to predict the noise epsilon_theta(x_t, t) at each step. Sampling starts from pure noise x_T ~ N(0,I) and iteratively denoises. DDPM uses 1000 steps; DDIM reduces to 50-100 steps with deterministic sampling. Classifier-free guidance scales: s*(epsilon_cond - epsilon_uncond) + epsilon_uncond.
